subtopic: Cells in Series and in Parallel
title: Cells in Series and in Parallel
summary: According to question, $$ I=\frac{5}{10}=0.5\ A $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{n\mathrm{R}+r} $$ Current, where, $r = \text{internal resistance of battery}$ $nR = \text{equivalent resistance of } n$ resistors in…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Net emf of the circuit is, $$ \mathrm{E}=10-5=5\mathrm{~V} $$ New resistance, $ R = 1 + 2 + 7 = 10\ \Omega $ Therefore, current, $ I = \frac{E}{R} $ When $n$ equal resistors of resistance $R$ connected in series, then Putting value of E and R in eqn. (i), we get current, Current will be directed from A to B through E. - According to question, $$ I=\frac{5}{10}=0.5\ A $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{n\mathrm{R}+r} $$ Current, where, $r = \text{internal resistance of battery}$ $nR = \text{equivalent resistance of } n$ resistors in series In question, $$ r=R $$ $$ \therefore $$ $$ I=\frac{E}{R(n+1)} $$ Similarly, when $n$ equal resistors of resistance $R$ are connected in parallel, then Current, $$ \Gamma=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\frac{\mathbf{R}}{n}+\mathbf{R}} $$ where, $ \frac{R}{n} $ = equivalent resistance of n resistors in parallel It is given that, $$ 10I=\frac{E}{\frac{R}{n}+R}=\frac{nE}{(n+1)R} $$ From (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \begin{array}{c}10\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}(n+1)}\right)=\frac{n\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}(n+1)}\\\Rightarrow\quad n=10\end{array} $$
##### Related Theory From given question, $$ I=\frac{E}{nR+R} $$ $$ 10I=\frac{R}{\frac{R}{n}+R} $$ Divide (ii) by (i), we get $$ 10=\frac{(n+1)R}{\left(\frac{1}{n}+1\right)R} $$ $$ n=10 $$ - According to the question, $$ \mathrm{E}-\mathrm{Ir}_{1}=0\mathrm{~and~I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}+\mathrm{E}}{r_{1}+r_{2}+\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\mathrm{E}}{r_{1}}&=\frac{2\mathrm{E}}{r_{1}+r_{2}+\mathrm{R}}\\\therefore\quad r_{1}+r_{2}+\mathrm{R}&=2r_{1}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{R}&=r_{1}-r_{2}\end{aligned} $$ - Reading of voltmeter = $ \frac{\frac{E_1}{r_1} + \frac{E_2}{r_2}}{\frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2}} = \frac{E_1 r_2 + E_2 r_1}{r_1 + r_2} $ On putting values, we get 14 volt.

title: Cells, EMF, Internal Resistance
summary: (i) When all bulbs are glowing, then, <div style="text-align: center;"><img…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - (i) When all bulbs are glowing, then,
Here, $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3}+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3}=\frac{2\mathrm{R}}{3} $$ $$ \because $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{i}=\frac{\mathrm{E}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}^{2}}{2\mathrm{R}} $$ (ii) Two from section A and one from section B are glowing,
Then $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}+\mathrm{R}\\=\frac{3\mathrm{R}}{2}\end{array} $$ $$ \therefore\text{Power,}P_{f}=\frac{2E^{2}}{3R} $$ ∴ From (i) and (ii), we get $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}_{i}}{\mathrm{P}_{f}}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}^{2}.3\mathrm{R}}{2\mathrm{R}.2\mathrm{E}^{2}}=9:4. $$
- The given situation can be represented as follows: $$ \begin{array}{c}0.2\mathrm{~A}\\2.1\mathrm{~V}\\\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{R}}\\0.2=\frac{2.1}{\mathrm{r}+10}\\\mathrm{r}=0.5\Omega\end{array} $$
- Terminal potential difference, $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{E}-\mathbf{I}r $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{E}-\left[\frac{\mathbf{E}}{\mathbf{R}+r}\right]r=\frac{\mathbf{E}\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{R}+r} $$ From given condition, $$ \mathrm{E}=2.2\mathrm{V} $$ and where, R = 5 Ω then potential, V = 1.8 V Therefore, $$ 1.8=\frac{2.2\times5}{5+r} $$ $$ r=\frac{10}{9}\Omega $$

subtopic: Drift of Electrons and The Origin of Resistivity
title: Drift of Electrons and The Origin of Resistivity
summary: For the original wire with diameter $d$, the current is $I_{1}=100\ m\ A=0.1\ A$, drift velocity $v_{d}=v$ $A=\pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ The current in original wire is $I_{1}=n\left(\frac{\pi…
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- For the original wire with diameter $d$, the current is $I_{1}=100\ m\ A=0.1\ A$, drift velocity $v_{d}=v$ $A=\pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ The current in original wire is $I_{1}=n\left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\right)ev$ For the new wire with diameter $=\frac{d}{2}$ $A'=n\left(\frac{\frac{d}{2}}{2}\right)^{2}=\pi\left(\frac{d}{4}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi d^{2}}{16}$ As $I=neAv_{d}$ $\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}=\frac{n\left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{16}\right)ev'}{n\left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\right)ev}$ $\frac{0.2}{0.1}=\frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{v'}{v}$ $\Rightarrow4\times2=\frac{v'}{v}$ $\therefore v'=8v$
- Given, $$ R=10\ \Omega,E=10\ V/m,l=10\ m $$ Radius, $$ r=\frac{10^{-2}}{\sqrt{\pi}}m $$ We know, $$ \begin{aligned}\rho&=\frac{RA}{l}=\frac{10\times\pi\times\frac{10^{-4}}{\pi}}{10}\\&=10^{-4}\Omega m\end{aligned} $$ Current density, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{J}&=\mathrm{E}\ \sigma=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\rho}=\frac{10}{10^{-4}}\\&=10^{5}\mathrm{~A}/\mathrm{m}^{2}\end{aligned} $$
- Drift velocity, $ v_d = \frac{eL}{m}\tau $ Current density, $ J = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{neAv_d}{A} = nev_d $ Resistivity, $ \rho = \frac{m}{ne^2\tau} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \tau = \frac{m}{ne^2\rho} $ Resistance, $ R = \frac{V}{I} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \rho \frac{l}{A} = \frac{EI}{I} $ $ \rho = \frac{E}{L} $
- We know that, $$ \mathbf{J}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{A}} $$ Drift velocity, $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{n A e}=\frac{\mathrm{J}}{n e} $$ Electric field, $ E = \rho J $ From eq. (i), (ii) and (iii), we confirm that current density, drift velocity and electric field depend on area of conductor. Even if the area of cross-section of conductor is non-uniform, the number of electrons are flowing.

title: Electrical Energy, Power
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - $ P_{X} = 1 \, kW $, $ P_{Y} = 2 \, kW $ For parallel combination of heaters. $ P_{parallel} = P_X + P_Y = 1 + 2 = 3 \, kW $ For series combination of heaters $$ \frac{1}{\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{series}}}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}+\frac{1}{\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{P_{s e r i e s}}=\frac{2}{3}\mathrm{~k W} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{parallel}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{series}}}=\frac{3}{2}\times3=\frac{9}{2}, $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{P_{series}}{P_{parallel}}}=\frac{2}{9} $$ - We know, $$ \mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}t $$ so, $$ \mathrm{H}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{100}t $$ $$ \mathrm{H}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{200}t $$ $$ \frac{H_{1}}{H_{2}}=\frac{200}{100}=2:1 $$ - Protective device are necessary to protect electrical appliances or equipment. From the given devices, fuse is used in electric circuit as a protective device, it helps in preventing excessive amount of current to flow in the circuit or from short circuiting. -
Current through bulb, $$ I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{500W}{100V}=5A $$ $$ R=\frac{130V}{5A}=26\Omega $$ - Given, Total fall of potential between two cities $$ V=8\times150=1200V $$ Total resistance = 0.5 × 150 = 75 Ω $ \therefore $ Power loss, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} = \frac{1200 \times 1200}{75} $ = 19200 W = 19.2 kW - According to the question, $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{R}_{1}=\mathbf{R},\mathbf{R}_{2}=5\Omega,\mathbf{V}=10\mathbf{V},\mathbf{P}=30\mathbf{W}\\&\therefore\quad\mathbf{P}=\frac{\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}+\frac{\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}\\&\quad\frac{10^{2}}{\mathbf{R}}=30-\frac{10^{2}}{5}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{100}{\mathbf{R}}=30-20\\&\quad\mathbf{R}=10\mathbf{O}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{R=10\Omega} $$ - We know power, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\Delta\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}\times100\%&=\frac{2\Delta\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\times100\%\\&=2\times2.5=5\%\end{aligned} $$ Power. - Voltage across all three branches are same.
Voltage across $ 2\ \Omega $ resistance, $$ \mathbf{V}=2\times3=6\mathbf{V} $$ So voltage across lowest arm, $$ \mathbf{V}_{l}=6\mathbf{V} $$ Current across 5 $ \Omega $ is, $$ I=\frac{6}{1+5}=1\ A $$ ∴ Power across 5 $ \Omega $ is, $$ \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}^{2}\mathrm{R}=(1)^{2}\times5=5\mathrm{~W} $$ - $ i_{1}=1\ \text{amp}=\text{current through}4\ \Omega $ $ i_{2}=\text{current through}3\ \Omega $ $$ \begin{aligned}&\therefore\quad\frac{i_{2}}{i_{1}}=\frac{4}{3}\\&\Rightarrow\quad i_{2}=\frac{4}{3}\text{A}\\&\quad i_{3}=i_{1}+i_{2}=1+\frac{4}{3}\\&\quad=\frac{7}{3}\text{A}\\&and\quad\frac{i_{3}}{i_{4}}=\frac{5/4}{12/7}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{7}{3}\times\frac{12}{7}=i_{4}\times\frac{5}{4}\end{aligned} $$
$$ i_{4}=\frac{16}{5}=3.2A $$ $ V_{NM} = P.D. $ across M and N $ = i_{4} \times 1 $ $ = 3.2 \times 1 = 3.2 \, Volt $ -
Total power dissipated, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} $ $ = \frac{18 \times 18}{6} = 54 \, \text{W} $ -
1 $ \Omega $ and 3 $ \Omega $ are in series. So, $$ \mathbf{R_{s}}=\mathbf{R}_{1}+\mathbf{R}_{3} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s}}=3+1=4\Omega $$ and $$ \mathrm{R}_{2}=8\ \Omega $$ i = net current in the circuit Current through $ R_{1} $ is, $$ i_{1}=\frac{i\times\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{s}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{i\times8}{12}=\frac{2i}{3} $$ Current through $ R_{2} $ is, $$ i_{2}=\frac{i\times\mathrm{R}_{s}}{\mathrm{R}_{s}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{i\times4}{12}=\frac{i}{3} $$ Power dissipated in 3 $ \Omega $ resistance is, $$ \mathbf{P}_{1}=i_{1}^{2}\times3 $$ Power dissipated in 8 $ \Omega $ resistance is: $$ \mathbf{P}_{2}=i_{2}^{2}\times8 $$ From eqns (i), (ii), (iii) & (iv), we get $$ \mathrm{\frac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}}={\frac{i_{1}^{2}\times3}{i_{2}^{2}\times8}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{2i}{3}\right)^{2}\times3}{\left(\frac{i}{3}\right)^{2}\times8}=\frac{3}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{1}=\frac{3}{2}\times\mathrm{P}_{2}=\frac{3}{2}\times2=3\mathrm{~W} $$ - $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}^{2}\mathrm{~R}\\&\mathrm{P}=25\times\mathrm{R}\\&\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{25}=0.04\Omega\\ \end{aligned} $$ - The resistance of each bulb is, $ \frac{V^{2}}{P} = \frac{(200)^{2}}{60}\Omega $ When three bulbs are connected in series, Then equivalent resistance is = $ \frac{3 \times (200)^{2}}{60} $. $$ \because $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{eq}}=20\mathrm{W}. $$ - In India, $$ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{(220)^{2}}{\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{v}}} $$ As $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{I}}=\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{V}} $$ $$ \frac{(220)^{2}}{R}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{R_{V}} $$ $$ \mathrm{R_{v}}=\frac{110\times110}{220\times220}\mathrm{R} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{4} $$

title: Kirchhoff's Rules
summary: By KVL in closed loop ABCDA, $$ \begin{aligned}-I\times4-I\times1+4-I\times1+2&=0\\-6I+6&=0\end{aligned} $$ <div style="text-align: center;"><img…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Given, We know, $$ i_{1}=i_{2}+i_{3} $$ and $$ i_{2}r_{2}=i_{3}r_{3} $$ $$ i_{2}=i_{1}-i_{3} $$ $$ (i_{1}-i_{3})r_{2}=i_{3}r_{3} $$ $$ i_{1}r_{2}=i_{3}\left(r_{2}+r_{3}\right) $$ $$ \frac{i_{3}}{i_{1}}=\frac{r_{2}}{r_{2}+r_{3}} $$ - By KVL in closed loop ABCDA, $$ \begin{aligned}-I\times4-I\times1+4-I\times1+2&=0\\-6I+6&=0\end{aligned} $$
##### Related Theory
$$ \frac{4+2}{4+1+1}=\frac{6}{6}=1A. $$ - The circuit diagram is given by, Applying KVL rule to Loop BCDEB.
$$ \mathbf{R}_{2}i_{2}+\mathbf{E}_{2}-\mathbf{E}_{3}-\mathbf{R}_{1}i_{3}=0 $$ - A $ \xrightarrow[2\mathrm{~A~}]{2\Omega} $ $ \xrightarrow[3\mathrm{~V~}]{1\Omega} $ B Applying Kirchhoff's law, we get, $ V_{B} = V_{A} - (2 \times 2) - 3 - (2 \times 1) $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ V_{A} - V_{B} = 9 V $ - The situation is shown in the given figure. then we use Kirchhoff's law
$$ 500\mathrm{I}+100\mathrm{I}=12 $$ $$ I=\frac{12\times10^{-2}}{6} $$ $$ =2\times10^{-2}A $$ $$ V_{B}=100\left(2\times10^{-2}\right)=2V $$ - Using Kirchhoff's law in path ACDB $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}+1+(1)(2)-2=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=1\text{volt}\end{aligned} $$ - Both statements (I) and (II) are correct. - Applying Kirchhoff's law, $$\Sigma V = 0$$ Here, $e_1 - (i_1 + i_2)R - i_1 r_1 = 0$ - Kirchhoff's first and second law of electrical circuits are consequence of conservation of charge and energy respectively. - Kirchhoff's first law applies to currents at a junction in a circuit. It states that at a junction in an electrical circuit, the sum of currents flowing into the junction is equal to the sum of currents flowing out of the junction. Kirchhoff's first law is in favour of the law of charge conservation. This is due to the fact that a point in a circuit cannot be both a source and a sink of charge.

title: Limitations of Ohm's Law
summary: From Ohm's law, $ R = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} $ If current decreases as voltage or temperature rises, then resistance is said to be negative.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - In discharge tube, the flow of positive ions and electrons causes the current. Moreover, secondary electron emission is possible. As a result of the non-linearity of the V-I curve, its resistance will be non-ohmic. - From Ohm's law, $ R = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} $ If current decreases as voltage or temperature rises, then resistance is said to be negative. In V-I graph, the current drops with rising voltage in graph segment CD. Thus, The negative resistance is represented by portion CD.
##### Related Theory The variation between Potential difference (V) and Current (I) through a conductor is represented by a straight line passing through the origin and having a constant slope, the reciprocal of slope of line gives resistance. - Given: $$ \mathrm{R}_{0}=2\ \Omega\ \mathrm{at}\ \mathrm{T}_{1}=0^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=6.8\ \Omega\ \mathrm{at}\ \mathrm{T}_{2}=80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ As, $$ \mathbf{R}=\mathbf{R}_{0}\left[1+\alpha\left(\mathbf{T}_{2}-\mathbf{T}_{1}\right)\right] $$ $$ 6.8=2\left[1+\alpha\left(80-0\right)\right] $$ $$ 6.8=2+160\alpha $$ $$ \alpha=0.03or3\times10^{-2}\circ C^{-1} $$ - As the temperature increases, the resistance of conductor increases and for semiconductor decreases, because temperature coefficient of resistance ($\alpha$) is positive for conductors and negative for insulators. ##### △! Caution Student may think that all the materials have identical dependence of resistivity on temperature but this is not true, as different materials have different dependence on temperature. - The negative temperature coefficient of the resistance is only present in the insulators or the semiconductors. In these, the resistance decreases with increase in temperature.
##### Related Theory Temperature Coefficient of resistivity is positive for metals (Copper) and is negative for Semiconductor (Silicon). - Specific resistance of a (conductor) increases with increase in the temperature.

title: Objective I
summary: The **Seebeck effect**: when two dissimilar metals (or semiconductors) are joined at two junctions kept at different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, a thermoelectric EMF is generated and a current flows in the closed…
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- **Ohm's law**: $V = IR$, i.e., voltage is directly proportional to current for a given conductor at constant temperature. $R$ is constant (does not depend on $V$ or $I$ for an ohmic conductor). This is an empirical law, not a universal one — many materials are non-ohmic. Answer: (B).
- From the microscopic definition of resistivity $\rho = E/J$ (field per current density): $$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$ Resistance increases with length (more collisions over a longer path) and decreases with area (more parallel paths for current). Answer: (B).
- In metals, current is carried by free electrons. At higher temperatures, lattice ions vibrate more vigorously, causing more frequent collisions with conduction electrons. This increases resistivity and hence resistance: $$R = R_0(1 + \alpha\Delta T) \quad (\alpha > 0 \text{ for metals})$$ For semiconductors, the opposite is true (resistance decreases with temperature). Answer: (B).
- In parallel, the same voltage $V$ is applied across each. Currents add: $$I = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2} = V\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$ $$R_{\rm eq} = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ This is always **less than** the smaller of $R_1$, $R_2$. Answer: (B).
- **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**: at any junction (node) in a circuit, the algebraic sum of all currents is zero: $$\sum I = 0 \quad \text{(currents in = currents out)}$$ This is a statement of **conservation of charge** — charge cannot accumulate at a node. Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) is the loop law (sum of EMFs = sum of potential drops), which is option A. Answer: (B).
- The Wheatstone bridge is **balanced** when $P/Q = R/S$ (or equivalently $PS = QR$). At balance, the potential at the two intermediate nodes B and D is equal, so no potential difference exists across the galvanometer — no current flows through it. This is used to measure an unknown resistance by adjusting others until null deflection. Answer: (B).
- Drift velocity: $v_d = \dfrac{I}{nqA}$. For copper: $n \approx 8.5 \times 10^{28}\,\rm m^{-3}$, $A \sim 1\,\rm mm^2$, $I \sim 1\,\rm A$: $$v_d = \frac{1}{8.5\times10^{28} \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 10^{-6}} \approx 7 \times 10^{-5}\,\rm m/s$$ This is of order $10^{-4}\,\rm m/s$ — extremely slow. Electric signals propagate at nearly $c$ because the electric field propagates, not the electrons themselves. Answer: (C).
- Current: $I = \mathcal{E}/(R + r)$. The terminal voltage (voltage across the external resistance) equals the EMF minus the voltage drop across internal resistance: $$V_{\rm terminal} = \mathcal{E} - Ir = IR$$ Both expressions are equal. The terminal voltage is less than EMF when delivering current (due to internal resistance loss). Answer: (B).
- Joule's law of heating: the electrical energy dissipated as heat is: $$H = I^2 R t = \frac{V^2}{R}\,t = VIt$$ All three forms are equivalent. The $I^2Rt$ form shows that heat is proportional to the square of current, the resistance, and the time. Answer: (B).
- Rated power $P = V_{\rm rated}^2 / R \implies R \propto 1/P$ at the same rated voltage. $$R_{60} = \frac{V^2}{60} > R_{100} = \frac{V^2}{100}$$ In series, the same current $I$ flows. Power in each: $P_i = I^2 R_i$. $$P_{60} = I^2 R_{60} > I^2 R_{100} = P_{100}$$ The 60 W bulb has higher resistance and dissipates more power in series — it **glows brighter**. Answer: (B).
- **Faraday's first law**: $m \propto Q = It$. $$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$ where $Z$ is the electrochemical equivalent of the substance. Doubling the current (or time) doubles the mass deposited. Answer: (C).
- **Faraday's second law**: for the same $Q$, $m_1/m_2 = E_1/E_2$ where $E = M/n$ is the chemical equivalent (molar mass $M$ divided by valency $n$, also called the gram equivalent mass). $$m = \frac{M}{nF} \cdot Q$$ where $F = 96485\,\rm C/mol$ is Faraday's constant. Answer: (B).
- The **Seebeck effect**: when two dissimilar metals (or semiconductors) are joined at two junctions kept at different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, a thermoelectric EMF is generated and a current flows in the closed circuit. This is the basis of the **thermocouple** used for temperature measurement. - Option A describes the **Peltier effect** (heat at junction when current flows). - Option C more precisely describes Peltier effect. - Option D describes Joule heating. Answer: (B).
- **Peltier effect**: when current flows through a junction of two different metals, heat is **absorbed** or **liberated** at the junction depending on the direction of current. It is the reverse of the Seebeck effect. Peltier coolers (thermoelectric coolers) use this principle — pumping heat from cold side to hot side using electrical energy. Answer: (B).

formulas.latex: m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t

title: Objective II
summary: The three true thermoelectric effects are: - **A** ✓: **Seebeck** — EMF due to temperature difference at junctions.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ✓: Series connection — single current path, same $I$ everywhere. - **B** ✗: Voltage divides: $V_i = IR_i$, different for different $R_i$ (unless all equal). - **C** ✓: $R_{\rm eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \ldots$ - **D** ✓: $R_{\rm eq} > R_{\rm max}$ (each resistor adds to the total). Answer: (A), (C), (D).
- - **A** ✓: KCL (sum of currents at node = 0) follows from charge conservation — no charge accumulates. - **B** ✓: KVL (sum of potential differences around a closed loop = 0) follows from energy conservation — potential is path-independent. - **C** ✗: KCL applies to both AC and DC circuits (charge is conserved in both). - **D** ✓: KVL: $\sum \mathcal{E} = \sum IR$ around any closed loop. Answer: (A), (B), (D).
- All four are correct forms of Joule's law $H = I^2 R t = V^2 t / R$: - **A** ✓: $H = I^2 R t \propto I^2$. - **B** ✓: $H = I^2 R t \propto R$. - **C** ✓: $H \propto t$. - **D** ✓: At constant $V$: $H = (V^2/R) t \propto V^2/R$. Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).
- The three true thermoelectric effects are: - **A** ✓: **Seebeck** — EMF due to temperature difference at junctions. - **B** ✓: **Peltier** — heat absorbed/liberated at junction when current flows. - **C** ✓: **Thomson** — heat absorbed or liberated in a single conductor carrying current while having a temperature gradient. - **D** ✗: **Joule** heating is not a thermoelectric effect — it occurs in any resistive material regardless of temperature gradients. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: Without fields, cathode rays travel in straight lines (evidence: shadow of obstacle). - **B** ✓: Deflected by $E$ (toward positive plate) and $B$ fields — proof of charge. - **C** ✓: Thomson showed $e/m$ is the same for all materials → electrons are universal. - **D** ✗: Cathode rays are **negatively** charged (electrons). Answer: (A), (B), (C).

formulas.latex: H = I^2 R t = V^2 t / R

title: Ohm's Law
summary: From question, $ R_{1}=R_{2} $ and $ l_{1}=l_{2} $ Resistance, $$ \mathbf{R}=\boldsymbol{\rho}\ \frac{\boldsymbol{l}}{\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ ↳\quad\downarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad $$ $$…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - From question, $ R_{1}=R_{2} $ and $ l_{1}=l_{2} $ Resistance, $$ \mathbf{R}=\boldsymbol{\rho}\ \frac{\boldsymbol{l}}{\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ ↳\quad\downarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{\mathbf{R}_{1}}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}=\frac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}.\frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}\\&\frac{\mathbf{R}_{1}}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}=\frac{l_{1}}{l_{1}}.\frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}\\&\frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}=1\\ \end{aligned} $$ [Using (i)]
##### Mnemonics ←Just REPLAY the video. $$ Resistance=\rho\left(\frac{Length}{Area}\right) $$ - In both the cases, Volume of material is same, $$ \circlearrowleft $$ $$ \mathbf{A}_{1}l_{1}=\mathbf{A}_{2}l_{2} $$ It is also given, $l_{2}=nl_{1}$ $$ \mathbf{A}_{1}l_{1}=\mathbf{A}_{2}n l_{1} $$ $$ \mathbf{A}_{2}=\frac{\mathbf{A}_{1}}{n} $$ Now, when the wire is stretched, then resistance $$ \mathrm{R}_{1}=\mathrm{p}\frac{l_{2}}{\mathrm{A}_{2}}=\mathrm{p}\cdot\frac{n l_{1}}{\mathrm{A}_{1}} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{1}=\mathbf{R}\mathbf{n}^{2} \quad \left[\because\mathbf{R}=\left(\rho\frac{l_{1}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}\right)\right] $$ - $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho l}{\mathrm{A}}=4\ \Omega $$ New length, l = 2l On stretching, volume remain constant. ∴ Resistance of stretched wire is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{R}^{\prime}&=\frac{\rho I^{\prime}}{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}}=\rho\frac{(2l)}{\left(\mathrm{A}/2\right)}=\frac{4\rho l}{\mathrm{A}}\\&=4\times4=16\Omega\end{aligned} $$ - After stretching, Specific resistance ( $ \rho $) will remain same. Original resistance of the wire $$ \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{p}\frac{l}{\mathrm{A}} $$ or $$ \mathsf{R}\propto\frac{l}{\mathsf{A}} $$ or $$ \mathrm{R}\propto\frac{l^{2}}{\mathrm{V}} $$ $$ \left(\mathrm{as}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{Al}\right) $$ and $$ \mathbf{R}^{\prime}\propto\frac{(l+10\%\times l)^{2}}{\mathrm{V}} $$ Therefore $$ \frac{\mathbf{R}^{\prime}}{\mathbf{R}}\ =\frac{\left(l+\frac{10}{100}l\right)^{2}}{l^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{r^{\prime}}{r}}={\frac{\left({\frac{11}{10}}l\right)^{2}}{l^{2}}}={\frac{121}{100}} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}^{\prime}=1.21\mathrm{~R~} $$ - Resistance of wire, $ R = \rho \frac{l}{A} $ $$ \mathrm{R}\propto\frac{l}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{l}{\pi r^{2}} $$ According to the question, when l and r are doubled, $$ \mathrm{R}_{1}\propto\frac{2l}{\pi(2r)^{2}} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{1}\propto\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{R} $$ Here, specific resistance of wire is independent of geometry wire, so it only depends on the material of wire. -
$ \therefore $ Voltage on 50 cm = $ \frac{6}{300} \times 50 $ = 1 volt - The relation between length and cross-section area of a wire is given by, $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho l}{\mathrm{A}} $$ where, $\rho$ is the specific resistance. It is the proportionality constant and depends on nature of material. (1) Length = L, cross-sectional area = A Putting in (i), we get $$ \mathbf{R}=\frac{\rho L}{\mathbf{A}} $$ (2) Length = 2L, cross-sectional area = $ \frac{A}{2} $ Putting in (i), we get $$ R=\rho\frac{2L}{A/2}=\frac{4\rho L}{A} $$ $$ Length=\frac{L}{2}, $$ cross-sectional area = 2 A Putting in Eq. (i), we get $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho(\mathrm{L}/2)}{2\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\rho\mathrm{L}}{4\mathrm{~A}} $$ It is clear from above, resistance is minimum only in option (B). - Given, We have, $$ \begin{aligned}l&=15\mathrm{~m}\\ A&=6\times10^{-7}\mathrm{~m}^{2}\\\mathrm{R}&=5\Omega,\rho=\?\\\rho&=\frac{\mathrm{RA}}{l}\\&=\frac{5\times6\times10^{-7}}{15}\\&=2\times10^{-7}\mathrm{\Omega}\mathrm{-m}\end{aligned} $$

title: Overview
body: **Key points from solved questions** - From the given circuit diagram - Here, $$ \mathbf{E}=\mathbf{I}\left(\mathbf{R}+\mathbf{r}\right) $$ and $$ \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{V}+\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}+r}\times r $$ $$ \circ\circ $$ $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{E}-\frac{\mathbf{E}}{\mathbf{R}+\mathbf{r}}\times\mathbf{r} $$ - Since, $ I = \frac{nE}{nr} = \frac{E}{r} $ So, I is independent of n. So, I is constant.

title: Wheatstone Bridge
body:
**Key points from solved questions** -
∵ Total resistance of wheatstone bridge, $$ R=\frac{(40)(120)}{40+120}=30\ \Omega $$
(A) 4 V (C) 12 V (B) 2 V
(D) 6 V
[AIPMT Screening 2012] 7. In the circuit shown in the figure, if potential at point A is taken to be zero, the potential at point B is:
(A) -1 V (C) -2 V (B) +2 V (D) + 1 V [AIPMT Mains 2011] 58. Consider the following two statements; (I) Kirchhoff's junction law follows from the conservation of charge. (II) Kirchhoff's loop law follows from the conservation of energy. Which of the following is correct? Choose the correct option: (A) Both (I) and (II) are wrong. (B) (I) is correct and (II) is wrong. (C) (I) is wrong and (II) is correct. (D) Both (I) and (II) are correct. [AIPMT Screening 2010] 59. See the electrical circuit shown in the figure. Which of the following equations is a correct equation for it?
(A) $ e_1 - (i_1 + i_2)R - i_1r_1 = 0 $ (B) $ e_{2}-i_{1}r_{2}-e_{1}-i_{1}r_{1}=0 $ (C) $ -e_2 - (i_1 + i_2)R + i_2r_2 = 0 $ $$ \boldsymbol{e}_{1}-(i_{1}+i_{2})\boldsymbol{R}+i_{1}\boldsymbol{r}_{1}=0 $$ [AIPMT Screening 2009] 60. Kirchhoff's first and second laws of electrical circuits are consequences of: (A) conservation of energy and electric charge respectively. (B) conservation of energy. (C) conservation of electric charge and energy respectively. (D) conservation of electric charge. [AIPMT 2006] 61. Kirchhoff's first law of electricity follows: (A) only law of conservation of energy (B) only law of conservation of charge (C) law of conservation of both energy and charge (C) law of conservation of energy and some other times law of conservation of charge [AIPMT 1992] ### 3.13. Wheatstone Bridge 62. Choose the correct circuit which can achieve the bridge balance. (A)
(B)
(C)
(D)
##### [NEET 2024] 63. The resistance of the four arms P, Q, R and S in a Wheatstone's bridge are 10 Ω, 30 Ω, 30 Ω and 90 Ω, respectively. The emf and internal resistance of the cell are 7 V and 5 Ω respectively. If the galvanometer resistance is 50 Ω, the current drawn from the cell will be: (A) 1.0 A (B) 0.2 A (C) 0.1 A (D) 2.0 A [NEET 2013] 64. Three resistances P, Q, R each of 2 Ω and an unknown resistance S form the four arms of a Wheatstone bridge circuit. When a resistance of 6 Ω is connected in parallel to S, the bridge gets balanced. What is the value of S? (A) 3 Ω (B) 6 Ω (C) 1 Ω (D) 2 Ω [AIPMT 2007]

title: reNEET2026
summary: In the series circuit (A) the $+\alpha$ and $-\alpha$ resistances change oppositely, so the total resistance stays $2R_0$ and $I_A$ is constant.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- In the series circuit (A) the $+\alpha$ and $-\alpha$ resistances change oppositely, so the total resistance stays $2R_0$ and $I_A$ is constant. In the parallel circuit (B) the net resistance decreases with temperature, so $I_B$ increases.
